idea:
Build a web app called Drop-mo that lets someone coordinate a surprise Venmo "drop" for a friend or family member hit by an emergency — basement flood, medical bill, job loss, whatever. The organizer creates a private campaign, shares it by email with friends and family, contributors pledge an amount, and on a chosen drop day everyone sends their pledged amount to the recipient's Venmo at roughly the same time. The recipient wakes up to a flood of support without ever knowing it was coordinated.
Origin story — preserve this feeling in every UX decision
A friend's basement flooded. I want to rally friends and family to help, but I don't want the recipient to know anyone's organizing behind the scenes. I want it to feel like spontaneous, overwhelming generosity from their whole network arriving at once. The surprise IS the product.
Brand voice

Name: Drop
Core verb: "Start a drop" / "Join the drop" / "Drop day"
Tagline candidates: "Rally the group. Surprise the one." · "Pull off the surprise." · "They feel the love. They never see it coming."
Vibe: Hype but considerate. Conspiratorial-in-a-good-way. Think: group chat energy, not charity-platform energy.


Tech Stack (non-negotiable)

AWS Amplify Gen 2 (TypeScript-first, code-defined backend)
Frontend: React + TypeScript + Vite
Backend: Amplify Data (AppSync + DynamoDB), Amplify Auth (Cognito), Amplify Functions (Lambda)
Email: Amazon SES via Lambda for invitations, reminders, drop-day triggers
Dynamic image rendering: Lambda + @vercel/og or satori + resvg-js for on-demand PNG generation (live email content)
Scheduled jobs: EventBridge Scheduler → Lambda for day-before and drop-day sends
Animation: Framer Motion (required — see Design)
Styling: Tailwind CSS + shadcn/ui
Hosting: Amplify Hosting


User Roles

Organizer — Cognito account (email/password + Google). Creates and manages drops.
Contributor — no account. Accesses drop via an unguessable signed link. Pledges and self-reports.
Recipient — not a user. Just a person with a Venmo handle. Never logs in, ideally never sees the app.


Core Flows
Organizer

Sign up / log in
Start a drop:

Recipient first name (no last name)
Recipient Venmo handle — single input, just @username (auto-strip any leading @ the user types)
Story (markdown textarea, feels like writing a letter)
Goal amount (optional)
Drop date + time (timezone-aware)
Personal note for the invite email


Review the auto-generated branded invite email (see Email System) — edit subject line, the personal note, or preview the live progress image before sending
Invite contributors by pasting emails (comma or newline separated) → Lambda batches SES sends
Watch dashboard: live-feed of pledges, progress bar, invitee status, post-drop-day "sent" status
Re-send invite / nudge pending invitees anytime before drop day
Edit / postpone / cancel any time before drop day

Contributor

Receive branded invite email (with live progress image)
Open the drop page — a single scrolling experience
Enter name (or mark anonymous), email, pledge amount, optional note
Receive confirmation email (branded, with live progress image)
Day-before reminder email
Drop-day email at the scheduled time containing:

Pre-filled Venmo deep link (handle + amount + note populated)
"I sent it" button that marks the pledge as completed


Can return to the page later to update status

Recipient

Gets hit with multiple Venmo payments on drop day. That's it.

Plan written: Engineer fixes both bugs (SVG template-literal-as-string in a rect width, and the main.tsx MIME/build-output mismatch), then QA verifies in-browser.

Two runtime errors to fix: (1) An SVG <rect> width attribute is being set to an unevaluated template literal string '${(1072 * pct) /…' — meaning a template string was passed as a plain string (likely missing backticks, wrong quote style, or a string concatenation bug) so React renders the literal source instead of the computed length. (2) main.tsx is being served with MIME type application/octet-stream, which means the production/preview server isn't mapping .tsx/.js correctly or the built asset path is wrong (often caused by a missing build, wrong base path in vite.config, or hosting serving raw source). Both are code-level fixes for the Engineer; QA verifies in the browser afterward. No design or architecture work needed.

Engineer is working: Fix two runtime errors:

1) SVG <rect> width error: 'Expected length, "${(1072 * pct) /…"'. Somewhere in the codebase a width attribute on a <rect> is being assigned a template literal as a plain string (quotes instead of backticks, or a JSX attribute like width={'${(1072 * pct) / ...}'} instead of width={(1072 * pct) / ...}). Grep for `1072 * pct` and `${` inside width=/height= attributes across .tsx/.ts/.js files (especially any progress bar, OG image, or SVG illustration components). Fix the interpolation so the attribute receives an actual number. Make sure the fix produces a finite, non-negative numeric width even when pct is 0 or undefined (clamp to [0, 1072]).

2) main.tsx MIME type error: 'Failed to load module script ... application/octet-stream'. This happens when index.html references /src/main.tsx (dev path) in a built/served context, or when Amplify Hosting / preview server serves the file without a proper Content-Type. Check index.html — the script tag should reference the built bundle in production, not /src/main.tsx. Verify vite.config.ts has correct `base` and that `npm run build` produces an index.html in dist/ whose <script type="module" src="..."> points to a hashed JS file under /assets/. If index.html in the repo root statically references /src/main.tsx (which is correct for Vite dev only), confirm that the deployed/served HTML is the one from dist/, not the source one. If hosting on Amplify, ensure amplify.yml's artifacts baseDirectory points at dist (or wherever Vite outputs).

After fixing, run `npm install` then `npm run build` until it exits 0. Do not introduce TODOs or placeholders.

The repo already has the correct structure: `dist/index.html` references the hashed bundle, `amplify.yml` deploys `dist`, and source `index.html` correctly references `/src/main.tsx` (Vite dev only). No React `<rect>` with the buggy template literal exists in src. However, I'll harden the Lambda SVG

Plan written: Engineer fixes both bugs (missing email trigger on drop creation + broken public page route/authorization), then QA verifies end-to-end.

Two related bugs reported by the user: (1) no confirmation/invite email is sent after creating a drop, and (2) the public drop page fails to open. Both are backend/frontend wiring issues — likely the SES Lambda isn't being invoked on drop creation and/or the public route + signed-link lookup is broken. This is a focused bug fix, so Engineer investigates and fixes both, then QA verifies end-to-end.

Engineer is working: Diagnose and fix two bugs: (1) After organizer creates a drop, no email is sent. Check the drop-creation flow — the SES Lambda (invite/confirmation) must actually be invoked. Verify the Amplify Function has SES permissions, the FROM address is verified (or sandbox mode is accounted for), env vars (e.g. SES_FROM_ADDRESS, APP_BASE_URL) are wired via secret() or envVars, and the frontend actually calls the mutation/function after Drop creation. Add a confirmation-to-organizer email at minimum, plus invites to any addresses entered. Surface failures in the UI rather than silently swallowing. (2) The public drop page won't open. Check the route (e.g. /drop/:token or /d/:slug) is registered in the React Router config, that the page component loads the drop by its unguessable token via a public (unauthenticated) Data query or Lambda, and that the authorization rules on the Drop model allow public read by token. Fix whatever is broken — missing route, missing authorization rule, missing query, or missing amplify_outputs.json guard. Run `npm run build` until it exits 0. Do not leave TODOs.

The user wants to confirm that sharing a drop link via text message (SMS/iMessage) produces a nicely branded link preview — the kind of rich card iMessage/Android show when you paste a URL. This is primarily an Open Graph / Twitter Card meta tag concern plus a dynamically generated OG image (the project already specifies @vercel/og or satori for live email images — the same Lambda can serve OG images). (3) On the organizer dashboard, add a prominent 'Copy link for text' button next to the existing share controls with a toast confirming 'Link copied — paste into iMessage, WhatsApp, or any group chat'. Include subtle helper text: 'Previews as a branded card in most messaging apps.' (4) Make sure the signed link itself is SMS-friendly: short enough to not wrap awkwardly, no tracking junk, https. Plan written. Single subtask to Installer: fix the duplicated mapping key in amplify.yml (preserving `pipeline-deploy`), verify the build, and push.

The Amplify build failed at YAML parsing: 'duplicated mapping key (9:1)' in amplify.yml. The previous fix attempt likely left two top-level keys (e.g., two 'phases:' or duplicate 'frontend:'/'backend:' blocks). This is a single-file fix — repair the YAML structure so backend phase runs `npx ampx pipeline-deploy` and frontend phase runs `npm run build`, then verify with a local build and push. One engineering subtask is sufficient.

Installer is working: Fix amplify.yml YAML parse error 'duplicated mapping key (9:1)'. Read the current amplify.yml — it has duplicated keys (likely two 'phases:' under one section, or duplicated frontend/backend blocks from the previous fix attempt). Rewrite it to the canonical Amplify Gen 2 structure with exactly one 'backend:' section (preBuild: npm ci; build: npx ampx pipeline-deploy --branch $AWS_BRANCH --app-id $AWS_APP_ID) and exactly one 'frontend:' section (preBuild: npm ci; build: npm run build; artifacts baseDirectory: dist, files: **/*; cache paths: node_modules/**/* and .npm/**/*). DO NOT remove or comment out `npx ampx pipeline-deploy` — it is required to generate amplify_outputs.json. Verify amplify_outputs.json is NOT committed (gitignored). Run `npm run build` locally until it exits 0 (note: it may fail locally if amplify_outputs.json is missing — that is expected and will be generated by pipeline-deploy in CI; if so, ensure the frontend code handles missing config gracefully per CLAUDE.md). Then: git add -A && git commit -m 'Fix: repair duplicated mapping key in amplify.yml' && git push.

Plan written. One Installer subtask: audit amplify.yml ordering + resource.ts secret usage, verify local build, and flag the IAM BootstrapDetectionError as a user-side AWS Console fix (not a repo fix, per hard rules).

The Amplify build failed with BootstrapDetectionError — the CodeBuild role lacks ssm:GetParameter permission for /cdk-bootstrap/hnb659fds/version. Per hard rules, we must NOT disable pipeline-deploy; this is an IAM/infra issue the user must resolve in AWS Console. However, we should still verify the local build is clean, confirm amplify.yml correctly orders pipeline-deploy before npm run build, double-check no process.env misuse in function resource.ts files, and push any warranted fixes so the next deploy attempt (after IAM fix) succeeds cleanly. One Installer subtask covers this audit + local build verification.

Installer is working: Diagnose the failed Amplify deploy. The root cause is a BootstrapDetectionError: the CodeBuild role is not authorized to call ssm:GetParameter on /cdk-bootstrap/hnb659fds/version. This is an IAM infrastructure issue, NOT a code issue — do NOT disable or remove `npx ampx pipeline-deploy` from amplify.yml (hard rule). Steps: (1) Read amplify.yml and confirm pipeline-deploy runs in the backend phase BEFORE `npm run build` in the frontend phase — fix ordering if wrong. (2) Audit every amplify/functions/*/resource.ts file; ensure any runtime secret uses `secret('NAME')` from '@aws-amplify/backend', never `process.env.X || ''`. Fix any violations. (3) Confirm amplify_outputs.json is NOT tracked in git and that `amplify_outputs*` is in .gitignore. (4) Run `npm install` then `npm run build` locally until it exits 0; fix any TS/build errors. (5) If any fixes were made, run `git add -A && git commit -m "Fix deployment errors (attempt 2)" && git push`. (6) In your final report, clearly state that the remaining BootstrapDetectionError requires the user to attach/repair the IAM service role on the Amplify app in AWS Console (grant ssm:GetParameter on arn:aws:ssm:*:*:parameter/cdk-bootstrap/* and CDK deploy permissions) — this cannot be fixed from the repo. If no code fixes were warranted, do not create an empty commit; just report.

Plan written. Root cause is an auth-mode mismatch on the invite-send mutation — Engineer fixes the Cognito/SES path (SES is the free, secure in-stack option), QA validates signed-in/signed-out and federated flows.

The 'No federated jwt' error means the frontend is calling an Amplify Data mutation/function using userPool auth while the user isn't signed in (or the call path requires IAM/public auth). The free, secure fix is to route invite sending through an Amplify Function (Lambda) that uses SES (AWS's free tier covers 3k emails/mo from Lambda) and is invoked via an AppSync mutation configured with proper authorization (userPool for organizers, or identityPool/iam where appropriate). The Engineer needs to audit the invite-send code path, fix the auth mode on the mutation/function invocation, ensure the user is authenticated when calling it, and wire SES with verified sender. One engineering subtask covers this; QA validates end-to-end.

Engineer is working: Fix the 'No federated jwt' error when sending invite emails. Investigation + fix:

1. Locate the invite-send code path (likely amplify/functions/send-invite/ or similar, plus the frontend call site in src/ where organizer clicks 'Send invites').
2. Root cause: 'No federated jwt' is thrown by Amplify when a call is made with authMode 'userPool' but no Cognito session exists, OR when a Lambda/resolver expects an identityPool/IAM credential it can't get. Determine which: (a) is the organizer actually signed in at the moment of send? (b) what authorization mode is declared on the mutation/function in amplify/data/resource.ts?
3. Fix approach (free + secure):
   - Ensure the send-invites mutation in amplify/data/resource.ts uses `allow.authenticated()` (Cognito user pool) so only signed-in organizers can trigger it. Do NOT expose it to public/apiKey.
   - In the frontend, pass `{ authMode: 'userPool' }` explicitly to the client.mutations.sendInvites(...) call, and guard the UI so the button is disabled until `useAuthenticator`/`getCurrentUser` resolves a signed-in user.
   - If the organizer is signed in with Google federation, confirm the Cognito User Pool has the Google identity provider wired and that Amplify Auth's `fetchAuthSession()` returns tokens before the mutation is called. If tokens are missing, force a re-auth redirect.
4. Email delivery (free, secure): use Amazon SES via an Amplify Function. SES sandbox is free and sufficient for testing (verify the sender email + any recipient in the sandbox via AWS Console). Production sending is $0.10 per 1k emails — effectively free at this scale. Configure:
   - amplify/functions/send-invite/resource.ts: defineFunction with environment { SES_FROM_ADDRESS: secret('SES_FROM_ADDRESS') }.
   - Grant the function's execution role `ses:SendEmail` on the verified identity (via backend.ts customResources or a resource override).
   - Function handler uses @aws-sdk/client-ses SendEmailCommand with From = verified address, To = contributor, HTML body = branded invite.
5. Do NOT use SMTP/nodemailer with hardcoded creds, do NOT use a third-party free SMTP relay — SES is the free + secure path already in our stack.
6. Add user-facing error handling: if sendInvites throws, surface a toast with the real message (not just 'No federated jwt'); if the user isn't signed in, redirect to /login instead of attempting the call.
7. Run `npm run build` until clean. Verify the mutation signature matches frontend call. Document in a short comment only if the auth flow is non-obvious.

Engineer is working: Fix two bugs: (1) 'Couldn't send invites: e.emails is not iterable' — inspect the sendInvites Lambda handler (amplify/functions/**) and its caller in the frontend. The error means the handler is receiving an argument shape where `emails` is undefined/null/string instead of an array. Likely causes: AppSync wraps mutation args under `event.arguments` but handler reads `event.emails`; or the client passes emails as a newline/comma string but the handler expects an already-parsed array; or schema defines emails as a single String instead of [String!]!. Normalize: in the handler, accept both `event.arguments?.emails ?? event.emails`, coerce a string to an array by splitting on commas/newlines and trimming, and guard with `Array.isArray` before iterating. Update the Amplify Data schema if needed so `emails: a.string().array().required()` (or equivalent) and redeploy types. Also ensure the client sends a real array, not a single concatenated string. (2) The public drop page is still not working — load the route that contributors visit via the signed link, reproduce the failure, and fix it. Typical culprits: unauthenticated data access not allowed by the model's authorization rules (need `allow.publicApiKey()` or a custom resolver via signed token), missing API key configuration, route not registered, or the page trying to call an authenticated query. Engineer is working: Fix two production bugs on the deployed Amplify site (main.d1u2km81dncoin.amplifyapp.com):

1) PUBLIC DROP PAGE 404 — /d/:token returns 'webpage can't be found'. Root cause is almost certainly that Amplify Hosting isn't rewriting unknown paths to /index.html for the Vite SPA. Fix by adding a SPA rewrite rule. Two acceptable approaches:
   a) Add a `customHttp.yml` or update `amplify.yml` with a redirect rule: source `</^[^.]+$|\.(?!(css|gif|ico|jpg|js|png|txt|svg|woff|woff2|ttf|map|json|webp)$)([^.]+$)/>` target `/index.html` status `200` (rewrite).
   b) Or write `public/_redirects` with `/* /index.html 200` (Amplify Hosting supports Netlify-style _redirects when placed in the build output).
   Verify the React Router (or whichever router is used) has a route registered for `/d/:token` that renders the public contributor page, and that the page does NOT require auth (contributors have no account — they access via unguessable signed link, per CLAUDE.md).
   Also check the route path casing matches what the invite email links to.

2) INVITE EMAILS NOT ARRIVING — the Lambda that sends invites via Amazon SES isn't delivering. Investigate and fix:
   - Confirm the send-invite Lambda (amplify/functions/<send-invite-or-similar>/resource.ts) uses `secret('...')` for any secrets and has IAM permissions for `ses:SendEmail` / `ses:SendRawEmail` on the FROM identity.
   - Confirm the FROM address (and domain if applicable) is verified in SES in the deployed region. If the account is still in SES sandbox, TO addresses must also be verified — surface this clearly and either (a) verify brandondezzo@gmail.com as a test recipient, or (b) document the SES production-access request.    - Ensure the invite email body contains the correct absolute URL to /d/:token using the deployed Amplify domain (not localhost), read from an env var like `APP_BASE_URL` (set via Amplify Hosting envVars, not hardcoded).

Plan written. Root cause: circular dependency between data and function nested stacks — fix is to add `resourceGroupName: 'data'` to the data-using Lambdas' defineFunction calls.

The Amplify deploy fails with CloudformationStackCircularDependencyError between data7552DF31 and function1351588B nested stacks. This happens when Lambda functions are used as data resolvers (or call data API) while also being referenced elsewhere, creating a cycle between the auto-generated function stack and data stack. The documented fix is to assign those functions to the data stack via resourceGroupName: 'data' in defineFunction. One engineer subtask — audit each amplify/functions/<name>/resource.ts (send-invites, schedule-drop, progress-image, drop-day, public-share), add resourceGroupName: 'data' to functions that interact with data/GraphQL, run npm run build locally, and commit + push.

Engineer is working: Fix Amplify CloudformationStackCircularDependencyError between data and function stacks. In each amplify/functions/<name>/resource.ts (send-invites-lambda, schedule-drop-lambda, progress-image-lambda, drop-day-lambda, public-share-lambda), add `resourceGroupName: 'data'` to the defineFunction call for any function that is used as a data resolver or calls the data/GraphQL API (which includes all of these, since they read/write drops/pledges). Do NOT disable pipeline-deploy. Do NOT commit amplify_outputs.json. Keep all runtime secrets using secret('NAME'), never process.env. After the edit, run `npm install` then `npm run build` until it exits 0. Then `git add -A && git commit -m "Fix: assign data-using Lambdas to data resourceGroup to break circular stack dependency" && git push`.
